text: The selective cleavage of C−C bonds and the complete oxidation of alcohols to CO₂ are two of the biggest challenges for next generation low temperature fuel cells, particularly direct liquid feed fuel cells, that can efficiently utilize the renewable energy resource of alcohol biofuel. Here we design ultrathin core/shell Au/Pt nanowires (NWs) with stepped clusters of Pt on a stepped gold core, which perform as very efficient catalysts for the total oxidation of alcohols. The catalytic performance is clearly dependent on the Au/Pt composition, and our optimum catalyst, Au₁.₀/Pt₀.₂ NWs with the highest density of active sites, i.e. ~75% stepped Au/Pt, gives a mass activity of 196.9 A/mgPt (32.5 A/mgPt+Au) for ethanol oxidation. It also catalyses the oxidation of all of the primary alcohols we tested including methanol, n-propanol and ethylene glycol. We used in situ XAS and IR to show that the catalytic sites involve a synergy between low-coordinated Pt sites and a stepped Au surface that can accommodate the intermediates involved in alcohol electro-oxidation reactions. DFT studies confirmed the activity enhancement of undercoordinated Pt-Au sites due to their lower activation energy barriers for the C−C bond cleavage. This study design core/shell interfaces with stepped structures between Au and Pt as high performance catalysts for the electro-oxidation of alcohols and thus points the way towards design of next generation of direct alcohol fuel cells.
